Design and Analysis of Industrial Experiments

Catalog Description

Design and Analysis of Industrial Experiments. (3-0). Credit 3. Fundamental theory,

concepts and procedures required for industrial experimental design, statistical

data analysis, and model building, with emphasis on engineering formulations and

applications; one-factor experiments with and without restrictions on

randomization, treatment comparison procedures, Latin and other squares,

factorial experiments, full and fractional two-level factorial experiments, blocking in

factorial designs, response surface methodologies and introduction to Taguchi

methods. Prerequisite: STAT 212 or STAT 601.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  STAT 212 or STAT 601.

Participants will be assumed to have basic knowledge in statistics, including

parameter estimation, hypothesis testing and confidence intervals. Read the pre-

course reading materials to refresh your knowledge.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Understand formulations, concepts, and analytical procedures for design and

analysis of industrial experiments;

Understand fundamental principles of design and off-line experimentation

techniques;

Apply design tools in industrial applications;

Evaluate data-collecting, and experiment planning capability.

Special Course Designation

None

Mike Hamada), 2021, John Wiley.

R statistical software package will be used for the example discussed in class, home

from  http://cran.r-project.org .  You will use this software for homework assignments.

Grading Policy

Grades assigned are A for 90%–100%, B for 80%–89.9%, C for 70%–

79.9%, D for 60%–69.9% and F for less than 60%.

Any disagreements regarding a grade received on any graded material

must be discussed within two business days of the return of the

graded material. No grade will be changed beyond the two business

days limit.

Grades will be maintained in the gradebook within Canvas.

Grading components:

Homework – 10%

Exam 1 – 20%

Exam 2 – 20%

Final Exam  – 30%

Group Project – 20%

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Homework will be released on Canvas and is due on the designated. Late homework

will be docked by 15% per every day it is late. No extensions will be made if the due

date is your job interview day/exam day/holiday/etc. For homework before two exams

(I will remind you which ones are those), no late homework will be accepted. If you

would like to have your homework or exam re-graded, you have to do so within 10

days from the time when the homework/exam is returned to you.

Description of the project tasks will be announced on Canvas. Project report is due on

the designated date. Late project report will be docked by 15% per every day it is late

Course Contents/Topics

Description of the project tasks will be announced on Canvas. Project report is due on

Course Schedule

Course Contents/Topics

1.  Basic principles and introduction to regression analysis

2.  Experiments with a single factor, analysis of variance

3.  Experiments with more than one factor, blocking, analysis of variance

4.  Factorial experiments at two levels, comparison with “one-factor-at-a-

time” plans, analysis of location and dispersion, choice of optimal

blocking schemes

5.  Fractional factorial experiments at two levels, maximum resolution and

minimum aberration for choosing optimal designs, choice of optimal

blocking schemes

6.  Orthogonal arrays

7.  Introduction to fractional factorial experiments at three levels

8.  Variation reduction, robust parameter design for product and process

improvement

Course Schedule

AI Statement

According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the

appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving

appropriate credit ( aggiehonor.tamu.edu ). You should credit your use of anyone else's words,

graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles. AI text generators (ChatGPT, Google

Bard, etc.) should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the

instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to:

Creating or revising drafts

Editing your work

Reviewing a peer's work

This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which

are acceptable.

Such use of AI text generators in this manner could be considered plagiarism and cheating

according to Student Rule 20. More information may also be found

at  https://aggiehonor.tamu.edu  or you may contact your instructor if you have questions.
